output:
# Source-Confounded COVID Detection: Promoting a Same-Source AUC into a Confound-Attributed, Anatomy-Localized Skill Profile

*A single same-source AUC for a COVID-19 chest-image classifier conflates lung-pathology signal with acquisition/source shortcuts, so it must be promoted to a confound-attributed, anatomy-localized, confusion-resolved skill profile that provably collapses back to the same-source scalar as recomputed.*

## Research Analysis

### 1. Background and current state of the art

At the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, deep-learning classification of COVID-19 from chest radiographs (CXR) and CT became one of the fastest-growing application areas in medical-imaging machine learning. The dominant template is simple: take a convolutional backbone (often a pretrained ResNet, DenseNet, or a bespoke "COVID-Net"), assemble a training set of COVID-positive images plus pneumonia and normal comparators, and report a single same-source figure -- AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, or F1 -- on a held-out split drawn from the same pooled data. Headline numbers routinely exceeded 0.95 AUC, and the field treated that scalar as the measure of the model's diagnostic skill.

That practice is the disease being diagnosed here. Three converging results show the scalar does not measure lung pathology. DeGrave, Janizek and Lee (2021, Nature Machine Intelligence) showed that high-performing radiographic COVID-19 detectors select "shortcuts over signal": saliency and generative analysis revealed the models keying on laterality markers, image edges, diaphragm/shoulder regions, and dataset-of-origin cues rather than pulmonary opacity, and performance collapsed under cross-source transfer. Maguolo and Nanni (2021) drove the point home by masking out the lungs entirely and still obtaining high AUC -- direct proof that extra-pulmonary pixels alone carry the discriminative signal, because COVID-positive and COVID-negative images were sourced from different datasets. Roberts et al. (2021, Nature Machine Intelligence) systematically reviewed roughly 2,212 papers on COVID-19 ML from imaging and found that NONE were of clinically deployable quality, citing "Frankenstein datasets" (positives and negatives stitched from different sources), duplicate and pediatric-vs-adult leakage, and near-absent external validation.

This is a concrete instance of the general shortcut-learning phenomenon characterized by Geirhos et al. (2020): networks exploit any feature that is predictive on the training distribution, including spurious acquisition cues, and a single in-distribution metric cannot tell shortcut from signal. The standard data sources amplify the risk: the Cohen et al. COVID-ChestXray-Dataset (a heterogeneous public COVID CXR collection) and the Wang et al. COVID-Net / COVIDx benchmark pool positives and negatives across origins, while comparator negatives are typically pulled from the RSNA Pneumonia Detection Challenge, NIH ChestX-ray14 (Wang et al.), CheXpert (Irvin et al.), and MIMIC-CXR (Johnson et al.) -- each with its own device population, projection mix, demographic profile, burned-in annotations, and label noise. The disease-confusion axis compounds this: COVID, bacterial pneumonia, influenza-A, and normal overlap visually, so a "COVID detector" trained on adult COVID positives versus pediatric pneumonia or normal negatives may really be a source/age detector. In short, the field reports one same-source scalar that provably conflates pulmonary-pathology signal with acquisition/source/demographic shortcuts.

### 2. Key findings and analysis

The named gap is Source-Confounded COVID Detection (SCCD): a single same-source AUC or accuracy scalar for a COVID-19 chest-image classifier conflates GENUINE pulmonary-pathology detection with NON-PATHOLOGICAL acquisition and source shortcuts -- acquisition device and portable-vs-fixed signatures, hospital or source-of-origin, burned-in laterality and text tokens, AP-vs-PA projection geometry, and patient demographics such as age. High AUC is achievable from extra-pulmonary pixels alone, so the headline number is not anchored to lung pathology.

Three pieces of evidence anchor the diagnosis. DeGrave et al. (2021) used saliency and generative analysis to show detectors key on off-lung cues and collapse under cross-source transfer. Maguolo and Nanni (2021) masked the lungs entirely and still obtained high AUC -- an existence proof that extra-pulmonary pixels carry the discriminative signal. Roberts et al. (2021) reviewed ~2,212 papers and found ZERO clinically deployable models, attributing failure to Frankenstein datasets, source-mixing, duplicate leakage, and absent external validation.

We promote the scalar by decomposing apparent skill along three axes. Pathology-attributable signal is what SURVIVES lung-segmentation confinement and cross-source transfer. Source-attributable signal is what SURVIVES on background-only input and COLLAPSES under source stratification. Their interaction -- pathology x source coupling, e.g. a site that over-represents severe COVID -- is reported as a third, separately labeled component, never folded into either main effect. A fourth, confusion axis resolves performance over {COVID, bacterial pneumonia, influenza-A, normal} so a binary COVID-vs-normal proposal cannot hide pneumonia/influenza overlap.

Background-only AUC is the PRIMARY quantifier of the shortcut because it is a within-source measurement: domain shift is held fixed, so any above-chance score directly bounds reliance on extra-pulmonary pixels. The cross-source train-on-A / test-on-B gap is only an UPPER BOUND, because it conflates the source confound with benign domain shift and case-mix; within-source estimators (background-only and source-stratified AUC) are therefore primary, and the transfer gap is a corroborating ceiling.

The framework is anchored by a B1 collapse: a single source, full unmasked image, with no intervention reproduces EXACTLY the same-source scalar as recomputed -- a by-construction identity that makes the lens a strict generalization of current practice rather than a competing metric. Two placebo legs validate the mechanism: a structure-destroying control (permuting source assignments within a matched cohort) must drive the reported source component to ~0, and an absent-mechanism regime (single-source, single-device, single-projection, demographically matched cohort) must show ~no source component with B4 collapsing toward B1. Both legs are required; together they prove the decomposition detects real confound, not noise.

### 3. Novel insights or recommendations

The recommendation is to replace the single same-source scalar with a confound-attributed, anatomy-localized, confusion-resolved skill PROFILE. The defensible novelty is NOT the claim that COVID classifiers use shortcuts -- DeGrave et al. (2021) and Maguolo and Nanni (2021) already diagnosed that. What is unclaimed is a structured decomposition with four properties no prior work jointly provides:

(a) a variance-attribution that separates pathology-attributable signal from source-attributable signal AND reports their interaction as a third, separately labeled component;
(b) a principled intervention battery (lung-segmentation-confined input, foreground/background dissociation, source/site stratification, cross-source transfer, demographic stratification, text/laterality-token removal) used as the DISENTANGLING INSTRUMENT that produces the attribution;
(c) a PROVABLE B1 collapse: under a singleton source with no intervention, the profile reduces by construction to exactly the same-source scalar as recomputed (with "published" reserved for numbers within reproduction tolerance);
(d) a dual placebo pair -- a structure-destroying control and an absent-mechanism cohort -- that validates the mechanism, both legs required.

Concrete recommendations to the field follow directly. First, report background-only AUC alongside every headline number, as the primary quantifier of extra-pulmonary shortcut reliance. Second, report source-stratified and demographic-stratified (age-band, sex) AUC, never a single pooled scalar. Third, ALWAYS label the pathology x source interaction term separately, so a coupled site does not inflate the pathology main effect. Fourth, demand the B1 collapse, so any proposed lens strictly generalizes current practice and stays comparable to the published number.

The deeper insight is that this is a reusable EVALUATION LENS, not a one-off audit. Any medical-imaging classifier whose positives and negatives are drawn from different sources, devices, or populations -- mammography across vendors, dermoscopy across clinics, retinal imaging across cameras, pathology across scanners -- inherits the SCCD failure mode and the same decomposition. Converting an opaque "0.97 AUC" into "X survives lung confinement and cross-source transfer; Y is source/device/token-attributable; Z is pathology-by-source interaction" turns a dead-end leaderboard number into an actionable diagnosis of what the model learned and where to intervene. That is the lasting, transferable contribution.

### 4. References and evidence

1. DeGrave, A. J., Janizek, J. D., and Lee, S.-I. (2021). AI for radiographic COVID-19 detection selects shortcuts over signal. Nature Machine Intelligence, 3, 610-619.
2. Maguolo, G., and Nanni, L. (2021). A critic evaluation of methods for COVID-19 automatic detection from X-ray images. Information Fusion, 76, 1-7.
3. Roberts, M., Driggs, D., Thorpe, M., et al. (2021). Common pitfalls and recommendations for using machine learning to detect and prognosticate for COVID-19 using chest radiographs and CT scans. Nature Machine Intelligence, 3, 199-217.
4. Geirhos, R., Jacobsen, J.-H., Michaelis, C., Zemel, R., Brendel, W., Bethge, M., and Wichmann, F. A. (2020). Shortcut learning in deep neural networks. Nature Machine Intelligence, 2, 665-673.
5. Cohen, J. P., Morrison, P., Dao, L., Roth, K., Duong, T. Q., and Ghassemi, M. (2020). COVID-19 image data collection (COVID-ChestXray-Dataset). arXiv:2003.11597.
6. Wang, L., Lin, Z. Q., and Wong, A. (2020). COVID-Net: a tailored deep convolutional neural network design for detection of COVID-19 cases from chest X-ray images (COVIDx benchmark). Scientific Reports, 10, 19549.
7. Radiological Society of North America (2018). RSNA Pneumonia Detection Challenge dataset. Kaggle / RSNA.
8. Irvin, J., Rajpurkar, P., Ko, M., et al. (2019). CheXpert: a large chest radiograph dataset with uncertainty labels and expert comparison. AAAI, 590-597.
9. Johnson, A. E. W., Pollard, T. J., Berkowitz, S. J., et al. (2019). MIMIC-CXR, a de-identified publicly available database of chest radiographs with free-text reports. Scientific Data, 6, 317.
10. Wang, X., Peng, Y., Lu, L., Lu, Z., Bagheri, M., and Summers, R. M. (2017). ChestX-ray8 / NIH ChestX-ray14: hospital-scale chest X-ray database and benchmarks on weakly-supervised classification and localization of common thorax diseases. IEEE CVPR, 2097-2106.
11. Ronneberger, O., Fischer, P., and Brox, T. (2015). U-Net: convolutional networks for biomedical image segmentation. MICCAI, 234-241.
12. Selvaraju, R. R., Cogswell, M., Das, A., Vedantam, R., Parikh, D., and Batra, D. (2017). Grad-CAM: visual explanations from deep networks via gradient-based localization. IEEE ICCV, 618-626.
13. Guo, C., Pleiss, G., Sun, Y., and Weinberger, K. Q. (2017). On calibration of modern neural networks. ICML, 1321-1330.
14. Collins, G. S., Moons, K. G. M., Dhiman, P., et al. (2024). TRIPOD+AI statement: updated guidance for reporting clinical prediction models that use regression or machine learning methods. BMJ, 385, e078378.
15. Vaya, M. de la I., Saborit, J. M., Montell, J. A., et al. (2020). BIMCV COVID-19+: a large annotated dataset of RX and CT images from COVID-19 patients. arXiv:2006.01174.
16. Tsai, E. B., Simpson, S., Lungren, M. P., et al. (2021). The RSNA International COVID-19 Open Radiology Database (RICORD). Radiology, 299(1), E204-E213.
17. Kermany, D. S., Goldbaum, M., Cai, W., et al. (2018). Identifying medical diagnoses and treatable diseases by image-based deep learning. Cell, 172(5), 1122-1131.
18. Bates, D., Maechler, M., Bolker, B., and Walker, S. (2015). Fitting linear mixed-effects models using lme4. Journal of Statistical Software, 67(1), 1-48.
19. Efron, B., and Tibshirani, R. J. (1993). An Introduction to the Bootstrap. Chapman and Hall/CRC.

## Proposed Research Idea (5q)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field (Machine Learning).

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can evaluating a deep-learning COVID-19 chest-imaging classifier with a single same-source AUC or accuracy scalar conflate genuine pulmonary-pathology-attributable signal with acquisition/source-attributable shortcut signal (device and portable-vs-fixed fingerprints, hospital/source-of-origin, burned-in laterality/text tokens, AP/PA projection, and patient demographics such as age), so that it must be promoted to a confound-attributed, anatomy-localized skill profile produced by an intervention battery (lung-segmentation-confined input, foreground/background dissociation, source-site-stratified and cross-source external evaluation, demographic stratification, and text/laterality-token removal) that reports pathology-attributable (helped) signal, source-attributable (shortcut) signal, and their interaction as a third labeled component across the COVID/pneumonia/influenza/normal confusion axis, and provably collapses back to the same-source scalar as recomputed under a singleton source with no intervention applied?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Solving SCCD matters because the headline number that the entire COVID-imaging literature reports is, as currently produced, clinically inert and potentially dangerous. Roberts et al. (2021) reviewed ~2,212 models and found ZERO of deployable quality; DeGrave et al. (2021) and Maguolo and Nanni (2021) explain why -- the scalar can be achieved from source/laterality/extra-pulmonary cues that have nothing to do with disease. A number that cannot be attributed to lung pathology offers false reassurance: a model that looks excellent in-distribution can fail silently on a new scanner, a new hospital, or a demographic it never shortcut on, exactly where a triage tool would be trusted. Replacing the opaque scalar with a confound-attributed skill profile is therefore a precondition for any responsible clinical deployment, not a refinement of it.

Second, the contribution is a reusable EVALUATION LENS, not a one-off audit of one paper. The confound-attributed skill profile -- pathology-attributable signal, source-attributable signal, and their interaction, localized to anatomy and resolved across the disease-confusion axis -- generalizes to ANY medical-imaging classifier whose positives and negatives are drawn from different sources, devices, or populations. The same lens applies to mammography across vendors, dermoscopy across clinics, retinal imaging across cameras, and pathology across scanners. SCCD names and operationalizes a failure mode the whole sub-field shares.

Third, the profile is aligned with the direction regulators and reporting standards are already moving. TRIPOD-AI (Collins et al., 2024) and FDA-style expectations for external, multi-site validation demand evidence that a model generalizes beyond its development source. A source-stratified, cross-source, demographically stratified profile is precisely the evidence those frameworks ask for, supplied as a structured measurement rather than a single optimistic point estimate, and paired with calibration reporting (Guo et al., 2017) that a scalar AUC hides.

Fourth, the scientific value: the profile converts an opaque scalar into an actionable diagnosis of WHAT the model learned. Instead of "0.97 AUC," a team gets "X of the apparent skill survives lung confinement and cross-source transfer; Y is source/device/token-attributable; Z is pathology-by-source interaction." That decomposition tells researchers where to intervene -- harmonize acquisition, balance demographics, remove tokens, or collect matched negatives -- and turns a dead-end leaderboard number into a roadmap for building models that actually detect disease. That is the lasting future-research impact. Finally, this profile is an evaluation instrument: it does not constitute a clinically validated diagnostic device and must not be used for patient triage without prospective, site-specific validation.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

This is hard because every naive shortcut for measuring "real" lung-pathology skill is itself confounded by the same acquisition/source structure it is meant to remove.

(1) Lung segmentation reliability ON DISEASED lungs. The whole framework leans on masking everything outside an automatically segmented lung field, but segmenters are trained mostly on healthy or mildly abnormal CXRs. On severe COVID lungs (dense consolidation, whiteout, effusion) the lung boundary is exactly where the disease is, so the mask itself becomes correlated with both disease and source (severe cases cluster at specific sites/devices). A biased segmenter that clips opacified regions re-introduces the confound it was supposed to excise: the "lungs-only" arm silently leaks source signal, or the "background-only" arm silently retains pathology. A single segmenter therefore cannot be trusted. Mitigation: run at least two independent segmenters (a U-Net lung-field model plus a second architecture), and report mask-attributable variance as an explicit component so segmentation disagreement is surfaced rather than hidden.

(2) The cross-source transfer gap CONFLATES confound with genuine domain shift and case-mix. Train-on-A / test-on-B drops can be caused by the source shortcut OR by real distributional differences (population severity, scanner calibration, prevalence). So the transfer gap is only an UPPER BOUND on source-attributable signal, never a point estimate. The PRIMARY estimators must be within-source: above-chance background-only AUC and source-stratified AUC, where domain shift is held fixed.

(3) The interaction term (pathology x source) is real, not a nuisance. Severity and site are correlated by referral and triage patterns, so lung pathology and source are statistically coupled. This cross-term must be estimated and labeled SEPARATELY and never folded into either main effect. Folding it inflates the pathology main effect and flatters the classifier.

(4) Attribution is not causal without controls. A drop under masking is suggestive but not proof that the source caused the score; only the dual placebo pair (label shuffle + matched single-source cohort) licenses the causal reading.

(5) Multi-source label noise, duplicate / near-duplicate leakage across the COVID datasets, and small COVID-positive sample sizes inflate variance and can manufacture apparent skill. Near-duplicate images shared across train and test sources turn memorized acquisition fingerprints into "performance."

Why each single number fails: a single same-source AUC cannot separate pathology from source by construction (B1). A single saliency map is anecdotal and is a secondary witness, not a quantifier. A single external-test number is the transfer gap, which is an upper bound contaminated by domain shift. Only the full variance-attributed profile, with multiple segmenters and both placebo legs, isolates the source confound honestly.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The defensible novelty is NOT the claim that COVID classifiers use shortcuts. That is established prior art: DeGrave et al. (2021) and Maguolo and Nanni (2021) already DIAGNOSED shortcut reliance, qualitatively and by existence proof (saliency that lands off-lung; high AUC with lungs masked). Conceding that plainly is essential -- asserting shortcut existence as our contribution would be false novelty.

What is unclaimed is the QUANTITATIVE, confound-ATTRIBUTED, anatomy-LOCALIZED, confusion-RESOLVED skill PROFILE that promotes the field's same-source scalar into a structured decomposition with four properties that no prior work jointly provides:

(a) A variance-attribution that separates pathology-attributable signal (what SURVIVES lung-segmentation confinement and cross-source transfer) from source-attributable signal (what SURVIVES on background-only input and COLLAPSES under source stratification) AND reports their INTERACTION -- pathology x source coupling, e.g. a site that over-represents severe COVID -- as a third, separately labeled component, never folded into either main effect. DeGrave and Maguolo give a binary verdict ("shortcuts present"); they do not apportion the headline AUC into helped/shortcut/interaction shares.

(b) A principled intervention battery (lung-segmentation-confined input, foreground/background dissociation, source/site stratification, cross-source transfer, demographic stratification, text/laterality-token removal) used as the DISENTANGLING INSTRUMENT that produces the attribution. Prior audits use a subset of these as one-off demonstrations; here they are a coordinated measurement protocol whose outputs are the profile components.

(c) A PROVABLE B1 collapse: under a singleton source with no intervention applied, the profile reduces by construction to exactly the same-source scalar as recomputed on the same single source (with "published" reserved for numbers within reproduction tolerance). This collapse guarantee makes the lens a strict generalization of current practice rather than a competing, incomparable metric -- a property no existing shortcut audit offers.

(d) A dual placebo pair validating the mechanism: a structure-destroying control (permute source assignments within a matched cohort -> the reported source component must vanish to ~0) and an absent-mechanism regime (single-source, single-device, single-projection, demographically matched cohort -> ~no source component, B4 collapses toward B1). Both legs are required; together they prove the decomposition detects real confound, not noise.

Closest prior art and why each leaves this cell open: DeGrave et al. (2021) and Maguolo and Nanni (2021) DIAGNOSE shortcuts but give no apportioned, collapse-guaranteed profile; Roberts et al. (2021) AGGREGATES failures across the field but audits study quality, not a per-model attribution; generic domain-generalization and external-validation papers MEASURE a transfer GAP that conflates confound with benign domain shift (so it is only an upper bound here, not an attribution); saliency / Grad-CAM audits (Selvaraju et al., 2017) LOCALIZE attention qualitatively but do not quantify variance shares or guarantee collapse. None delivers a collapse-guaranteed, interaction-resolved, anatomy-localized attribution profile. That joint cell is the contribution.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

SUBJECTS (provenance-controlled). The released COVID-Net checkpoint (scored post-hoc, no retraining) plus three subjects we re-train ourselves from standard backbones (ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B0) so we control the training-data provenance for the controlled foreground/background arms. Controlling provenance is what lets us run the lungs-only-trained and background-only-trained controls cleanly.

DATASETS (all real, public). COVID-positive / mixed: Cohen COVID-ChestXray-Dataset; Wang COVID-Net / COVIDx; BIMCV-COVID19 (Vaya et al., 2020); RICORD (Tsai et al., 2021, RSNA/STR-curated COVID CT/CXR). Comparators / negatives / pneumonia: RSNA Pneumonia Detection Challenge; CheXpert; MIMIC-CXR; NIH ChestX-ray14; Kermany pediatric pneumonia (Kermany et al., 2018 -- the canonical "Frankenstein negative," adult-COVID-positive vs pediatric-normal age artifact). Metadata coverage: MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert provide view/projection (AP/PA) and some demographics; NIH and RSNA provide view and age/sex; BIMCV and RICORD provide site/device; Kermany provides age band (pediatric) by construction; Cohen provides source/clinical fields per image. Source/site, device, projection, and age-band become the framework factors.

METHOD PIPELINE.
(1) Lung segmentation: two independent segmenters (U-Net lung-field model + a second lung-segmentation network) produce masks; disagreement feeds mask-attributable variance.
(2) Intervention battery, mostly post-hoc re-scoring of released checkpoints: full image; lungs-only (everything outside mask zeroed/inpainted); background-only (lungs masked out) -- primary shortcut quantifier; text/laterality-token removal (detect + inpaint burned-in R/L markers and text); source/site-stratified; demographic (age-band, sex) stratified; cross-source external transfer. Each arm yields a re-scored AUROC/AUPRC.
(3) Attribution model: a crossed random-effects / variance-components decomposition (linear mixed model, REML; Bates et al., 2015) over factors {pathology-present (proxied via masking), source/site, device, projection, age-band}, with the pathology x source INTERACTION as a separate labeled term. Output = variance-share breakdown (pathology-attributable vs source-attributable vs interaction) plus a confusion-resolved profile over {COVID, bacterial pneumonia, influenza-A/viral, normal} so a binary COVID-vs-normal subject cannot hide pneumonia/influenza overlap.

BASELINE LADDER.
B1 (collapse anchor): single source, full unmasked image, no intervention -> reproduces the same-source AUC AS RECOMPUTED on that single source. This is a unit-testable by-construction identity (singleton source + zero interventions == single-source AUC); reserve "published" only for numbers within reproduction tolerance.
B2: add text/laterality-token removal + AP/PA conditioning (re-scoring only).
B3: add lung-segmentation confinement + foreground/background dissociation.
B4 (full profile): source-site stratification + cross-source transfer + demographic stratification -> the confound-attributed, anatomy-localized, confusion-resolved profile.

DUAL PLACEBO PAIR (both legs required).
(i) Structure-destroying: shuffle pathology-vs-source labels (permute source assignments within a matched cohort) -> the source-attributable component must vanish to ~0; if not, the decomposition is fitting noise.
(ii) Absent-mechanism: a single-source, single-device, single-projection, demographically-matched cohort where the shortcut cannot exist -> ~no source component and B4 collapses toward B1.

METRICS. AUROC and AUPRC per stratum and per intervention; background-only AUC as the PRIMARY shortcut quantifier; variance-share percentages (pathology / source / interaction) with stratified-bootstrap confidence intervals (Efron and Tibshirani, 1993); rank-stability of subjects across the profile versus the naive scalar ranking (P(rank=1), rank CIs); Grad-CAM in-lung attention fraction as a SECONDARY witness only. All demographic results are reported per stratum (age-band and sex) with confidence intervals and are never pooled away; no subgroup is suppressed in aggregate reporting, giving an explicit fairness-floor and disaggregated-reporting commitment.

COMPUTE (modest, arithmetic-consistent). Most arms are post-hoc re-scoring of released checkpoints = no training. Only the re-trained subjects and their foreground- and background-trained controls need training: 3 subjects x 3 training variants (full / lungs-only / background-only) x 3 provenance-controlled sources = 27 training runs at ~6 GPU-hr each = ~162 GPU-hr; add ~40 GPU-hr for two-segmenter mask inference plus all post-hoc re-scoring -> ~200 GPU-hr total on a single modern GPU.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES. We predict that a substantial share of headline COVID AUC is source-attributable: the background-only arm will score well above chance, and confining input to the segmented lung field will produce a large AUC drop. We expect classifier RANKINGS to reorder once confound-corrected -- subjects that lead on the naive same-source scalar will fall when source-attributable variance is removed and the COVID/pneumonia/influenza/normal confusion is resolved, so rank-stability under the profile will diverge from the scalar ranking. We expect both placebo legs to confirm the mechanism: label shuffling drives the source component to ~0, and the matched single-source cohort shows ~no source component with B4 collapsing toward B1. We expect the profile to collapse exactly to the same-source scalar (as recomputed) at B1, validating the unit-test identity, and we expect mask-attributable variance from the two segmenters to be reported, not hidden.

FALSIFIER: the hypothesis is REFUTED if background-only AUC is near chance AND classifier rankings do not reorder under confound correction (and the source variance-share is small with CIs overlapping zero). That outcome would mean the headline AUC is anchored to lung pathology, the source confound is negligible for these subjects, and the naive scalar was adequate. This is a genuine, pre-registered way to be wrong.
